aliases: Crispy Hollow Puri with Spiced Water

english: Crispy Hollow Puri with Spiced Water

food_subgroup: Deep-fried street snack with spiced filling and flavoured water

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Pani Puri. FOODON_03302050 (fried snack food) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Pani Puri. A0CKP (Snacks, savoury) is the closest defensible parent category in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Pani Puri or a sufficiently close parent food concept.

serving.description: 1 serving of 6 puris with filling and pani
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Pani Puri consists of 6 hollow crispy puris, each filled with spiced mashed potato or chickpea mixture and dipped in tangy tamarind-mint spiced water, weighing approximately 120 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Pani Puri (Golgappa) — Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, ICMR-NIN
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides values for puri (deep-fried semolina/wheat bread) and chaat-type snacks. Pani Puri nutrient values are derived by combining ICMR-NIN data for fried puri shells (~55% of serving weight) with boiled potato/chickpea filling (~35%) and spiced tamarind water (~10%), consistent with standard recipe proportions reported in Indian food composition literature. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate values are cross-referenced against USDA SR Legacy entries for semolina, whole wheat flour, and chickpeas to confirm plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Pani Puri exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated based on the closest analogous foods: deep-fried wheat/semolina puri (GI ~70, University of Sydney GI Database) and boiled chickpeas (GI ~28, Atkinson et al. 2021). The composite GI of 65 reflects the weighted contribution of the fried puri shell (~55% of serving, GI ~70) and the chickpea/potato filling (~35%, GI ~50–55) and spiced water (~10%, negligible glycemic contribution), yielding a weighted mean GI of approximately 65, classified as medium. Glycemic load per 120 g serving = (65 × 34.5 × 1.2) / 100 = 26.9, rounded to 27.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~504 mg per standard 120 g serving) from black salt, chaat masala, and tamarind water
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Pani Puri consumption due to its high sodium load from spiced water and seasoning blends; a single serving can contribute over 20% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~65) and moderate glycemic load (~27 per serving) driven by refined semolina puri shells
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Pani Puri in moderation; the medium GI and significant glycemic load per serving can cause a meaningful postprandial glucose rise, and portion control to 3–4 puris per occasion is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content from deep-frying (~8.5 g fat per 100 g) and high sodium from spiced water and seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Pani Puri poses a moderate concern due to its deep-fried preparation contributing to total fat intake and its high sodium content, which together may adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles when consumed frequently.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pani Puri (also known as Golgappa, Puchka, or Gupchup depending on the region of India) is one of the most popular street foods of the Indian subcontinent. It consists of small, hollow, crispy spheres (puris) made from semolina or whole wheat flour that are deep-fried until they puff up and become light and crunchy. Each puri is punctured at the top and filled with a spiced mixture of mashed boiled potato, cooked chickpeas or sprouted moong, and tangy tamarind chutney, then immediately dipped into or filled with chilled spiced water (pani) flavoured with mint, coriander, green chilli, cumin, black salt, and chaat masala. The combination of textures — the crisp shell giving way to the soft filling and the burst of tangy, spicy, refreshing water — is the defining sensory experience of this dish. Pani Puri is typically consumed in one or two bites per piece and is served in sets of 6 to 8 puris. Nutritionally, a standard serving of 6 puris (~120 g) provides approximately 276 kcal, 6.2 g protein, 10.2 g fat, and 41.4 g carbohydrate. The dish has a medium glycemic index (~65) due to the refined semolina shell, and a glycemic load of approximately 27 per serving, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is notably high (~504 mg per serving) from the spiced water and seasoning blends, which is a concern for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and dairy-free, and is widely available across Indian restaurants and street food stalls in Singapore. In Singapore, it is served at Indian cuisine restaurants and is a popular item during festive occasions and casual dining.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Pani Puri?
Is Pani Puri high in sodium?
What is the difference between Pani Puri and Golgappa?

confidence_reason: ICMR-NIN (2017) provides the primary nutritional basis for Indian fried snacks and chaat-type foods. No single ICMR-NIN entry exists specifically for Pani Puri as a composite dish; values are adapted from component-level data (fried puri, boiled chickpeas, potato, spiced water) consistent with standard recipe proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) using the closest matching foods. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) using component-level data for fried semolina/wheat puri, boiled chickpeas, boiled potato, and tamarind-mint spiced water, weighted by standard recipe proportions. Cross-referenced against USDA SR Legacy for plausibility. 2. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Pani Puri exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 3. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as Pani Puri is a plant-based food containing no animal products; this is naturally zero. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based fried snack with no fortification; naturally zero for this food type. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as the dish is typically prepared with vegetable oil (no partially hydrogenated fats); confirmed negligible per ICMR-NIN guidance. 7. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as Pani Puri contains no animal-derived ingredients; naturally zero. 8. Cuisine tags in the input (Curry, Naan, Butter Chicken) are not applicable to Pani Puri and have been disregarded; they appear to be restaurant-level tags from field survey rather than dish-specific descriptors. 9. Price range SGD 11.50–13.50 preserved from field survey source for reference but not used in nutritional scoring. 10. Chinese name 水球小吃 is a descriptive translation (water-ball snack) used in Singapore Chinese-language contexts; no single universally standardised Chinese name exists for Pani Puri.
